Search results: 



--- SOURCE 1: Industrial Enzyme - an overview ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/industrial-enzyme

SUMMARY:
Various molecular techniques have also been applied to improve the quality and performance of microbial enzymes for their wider applications in many industries (Chirumamilla et al., 2001; Nigam, 2013). In the food industry, amylolytic enzymes have a large scale of applications, such as the production of glucose syrups, maltose syrup, reduction of viscosity of sugar syrups, to produce clarified fruit juice for longer shelf-life, solubilization of starch in the brewing industry (Pandey et al., 2000b). Directed evolution has been applied to a number of microbial enzyme proteins such as bacterial proteases (Zhao and Arnold 1999) and lipases (Liebeton _et al._ 2000), and commercial companies, e.g. Diversa Corporation (San Diego, CA, USA) are capitalizing on the technology.

Industrial Enzyme
=================

In subject area:[Neuroscience](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience)

Industrial enzymes are defined as proteins used in various industrial processes, such as manufacturing, bioenergy, and biomaterials, to enable the development of sustainable products and processes. They are tailored through protein engineering to have specific activities for different industrial applications.

AI generated definition based on:[Comprehensive Biotechnology (Second Edition), 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/B9780080885049001823)

Chapters and Articles
---------------------

You might find these chapters and articles relevant to this topic.

Chapter

[Industrial Biotechnology and Commodity Products](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/B9780080885049001823)
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[2011, Comprehensive Biotechnology (Second Edition)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/referencework/9780080885049/comprehensive-biotechnology)D. Zhu, ... N. Wang

### Abstract

Industrial biotechnology is the application of biotechnology for industrial purposes, including manufacturing, [bioenergy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/bioenergy "Learn more about bioenergy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and biomaterials. Many see industrial biotechnology as the next wave of the biotechnology revolution. One of the important aspects of industrial biotechnology is the wide application of [enzymes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/enzyme "Learn more about enzymes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in various industrial products and processes, such as chemical, detergent, textile, food, animal-feed, [leather](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/leather "Learn more about leather from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and pulp and paper industries, enabling the development of sustainable products and processes. In the past few decades, the development in [recombinant DNA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/recombinant-dna "Learn more about recombinant DNA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") technology and advanced bioprocesses has made it possible to produce enzymes as purified, well-characterized preparations on a large scale. The latest developments in protein engineering and site-directed evolution have further enabled us to tailor-make enzymes with new activities and/or for new process conditions. This leads to a highly diversified industry that is currently growing both in terms of size and complexity. This article covers the major [industrial enzyme](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/industrial-enzyme "Learn more about industrial enzyme from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") types and their various applications, which will continue to play an important role in maintaining and enhancing the quality of life we enjoy today while saving the resources and protecting the environment for generations to come.

[View chapter](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/B9780080885049001823)[Explore book](https://www.sciencedirect.com/referencework/9780080885049)

Reference work[2011, Comprehensive Biotechnology (Second Edition)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/referencework/9780080885049/comprehensive-biotechnology)D. Zhu, ... N. Wang

Chapter

[Microbial Enzymes of Use in Industry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/B978012803725600011X)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[2017, Biotechnology of Microbial Enzymes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/book/9780128037256/biotechnology-of-microbial-enzymes)Xiangyang Liu, Chandrakant Kokare

### 11.4 Applications of Microbial Enzymes

Microbial [enzymes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/enzyme "Learn more about enzymes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are of great importance in the development of industrial bioprocesses. The end use market for [industrial enzymes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/industrial-enzyme "Learn more about industrial enzymes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is extremely widespread with numerous industrial applications (Adrio and Demain, 2014). Over 500 industrial products are being made using different [microbial enzymes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/microbial-enzyme "Learn more about microbial enzymes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (Kumar and Singh, 2013). The demand for industrial enzymes is on a continuous rise driven by a growing need for sustainable solutions.

Microbes are one of the largest and most useful sources of many enzymes (Demain and Adrio, 2008). A large number of new enzymes have been designed with the input of protein-engineering and [metagenomics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/metagenomics "Learn more about metagenomics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Various molecular techniques have also been applied to improve the quality and performance of microbial enzymes for their wider applications in many industries (Chirumamilla et al., 2001; Nigam, 2013). Many microorganisms including bacteria, [actinomycetes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/actinobacteria "Learn more about actinomycetes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and fungi have been globally studied for the synthesis of economically viable preparations of various enzymes for commercial applications (Pandey et al., 1999). The special characteristics of enzymes are exploited for their commercial interest and industrial applications (Table 11.1), which include thermotolerance, tolerance to a wide range of pH, and stability of [enzyme activity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/enzyme-activity "Learn more about enzyme activity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") over a harsh reaction conditions.

Table 11.1. Microbial Enzymes and Their Applications

| Industry | Name of enzymes | Applications | References |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Food, dairy and beverage | Protease, lipase, lactase, pectinase, amylase, laccase, amyloglucosidase, phospholipase | Degradation of starch and proteins into sugars, Production of low caloric beer, Fruit juice processing, Cheese production, Glucose production from lactose, Dough stability and Conditioning. | Gurung et al. (2013) and Nigam and Singh (1995) |
| Detergents | Amylase, cellulase, lipase, protease, mannanase | Remove protein after staining, Cleaning agents, Removing insoluble starch, fats and oils, To increase effectiveness of detergents. | Pandey et al. (2000a) and Wintrode et al. (2000) |
| Textiles | Amylase, cellulase, pectinase, catalase, protease, peroxidase, keratinase | Fabric finishing in denims, Wool treatment, Degumming of raw silk (biopolishing), Cotton softening. | Liu et al. (2013) and Saha et al. (2009) |
| Animal feed | Phytase, xylanase | Increase total phosphorus content for growth, Digestibility | Mitidieri et al. (2006) and Tomschy et al. (2000) |
| Ethanol production | Cellulase, ligninase, mannanase | Formation of ethanol | Jolly (2001) |
| Paper and pulp | Amylase, lipase, protease, cellulase, hemicellulase, esterase, ligninase, xylanase | Degrade starch to lower viscosity, aiding sizing, deinking, and coating paper. Cellulase and hemicellulase smooth fibers, enhance water drainage, and promote ink removal. Lipases reduce pitch and ligninase remove lignin to soften paper. | Kirk et al. (2002), Kohli et al. (2001), and Polizeli et al. (2005) |
| Leather | Protease, lipase | Unhearing, bating, depicking | Parameswaran et al. (2013) and Saha et al. (2009) |
| Pharmaceuticals | Penicillin acylase, peroxidase | Synthesis of semisynthetic antibiotics, Antimicrobials | Neelam et al. (2013) and Roberts et al. (2007) |
| Molecular biology | DNA ligase, restriction enzymes, polymerase | Manipulate DNA in genetic engineering. DNA restriction and the polymerase chain reaction. Important in forensic science. | Nigam (2013) and Roberts et al. (2010) |

The majority of currently used industrial enzymes are hydrolytic and they are used for the degradation of various natural substances. Proteases are one of most important classes of enzymes for the detergent and dairy industries. Carbohydrases, primarily [amylases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/amylase "Learn more about amylases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [cellulases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/cellulase "Learn more about cellulases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), used in industries such as the starch, textile, detergent, and baking industries, represent the second largest group (Underkofler et al., 1958). The fastest growth over the past decade has been seen in the baking and animal feed industries, but growth is also being generated from applications established in a wealth of other industries spanning from organic synthesis to paper and pulp and personal care. The use of enzymes in animal [nutrition](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/food-science/nutrition "Learn more about nutrition from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is important and growing, especially for [pig](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/pig "Learn more about pig from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and poultry nutrition.

[Enzymes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/enzyme "Learn more about Enzymes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") play key roles in numerous biotechnology products and processes that are commonly encountered in the production of food and beverages, detergents, clothing, paper products, transportation fuels, pharmaceuticals, and monitoring devices (Gurung et al., 2013). As the industrial [enzyme](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/enzyme "Learn more about enzyme from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") market has expanded at a rate of about 10% annually, microbial enzymes have largely replaced the traditional plant and animal enzymes. DNA technology has been used to modify [substrate specificity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/substrate-specificity "Learn more about substrate specificity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and improve stability properties of enzymes for increasing yields of enzyme-catalyzed reactions. Enzymes can display regional [stereospecificity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/stereospecificity "Learn more about stereospecificity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), properties that have been exploited for [asymmetric synthesis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/asymmetric-synthesis "Learn more about asymmetric synthesis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and racemic resolution. Chiral selectivities of enzymes have been employed to prepare enantiomerically pure pharmaceuticals, agrochemicals, chemical [feedstock](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/feedstock "Learn more about feedstock from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [food additives](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/food-science/food-additive "Learn more about food additives from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

#### 11.4.1 Food and Beverage

In the 20th century, enzymes began to be isolated from living cells, which led to their large-scale commercial production and wider application in the food industry. Food and beverage enzymes constitute the largest segment of industrial enzymes with revenues of nearly $1.2 billion in 2011 which is expected to grow to $2.0 billion by 2020. Enzymes used in food can be divided into food additives and processing aids. Most food enzymes are considered as processing aids used during the manufacturing process of foodstuffs (Saha et al., 2009) with only a few used as additives, such as [lysozyme](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/lysozyme "Learn more about lysozyme from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [invertase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/beta-fructofuranosidase "Learn more about invertase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The applications of different enzymes in the food industry are shown in Table 11.2.

Table 11.2. Applications of Enzymes in the Food Industry

| Process | Enzyme | Applications | References |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Baking | Amylase, protease | Conversion of sugar into ethanol and CO 2. To prepare bread | Collar et al. (2000) |
| Brewing | Amylase, protease | Conversion of sugar into ethanol and CO 2. To prepare alcoholic drink | Pandey et al. (2000a) |
| Corn syrup | Amylase | Preparation of low dextrose equivalent syrups | Kirk et al. (2002) |
| Cheese making | Rennin, lipases | Milk clotting, Flavor production | Okanishi et al. (1996) |
| Baby foods | Trypsin | Digestion | Parameswaran et al. (2013) |
| Coffee | Pectinase | Coffee bean fermentation | Kirk et al. (2002) |
| Dairy industry | Protease, lactase lactoperoxidase | Preparation of protein hydrolysates, preparation of milk and ice cream, Cold sterilization of milk | Tucker and Woods (1995) |
| Fruit juices | Glucose oxidase, pectinase | Oxygen removal, clarification of fruit juices | Godfrey and West (1996,b) |
| Soft drinks | Glucose oxidase | Stabilization | Kirk et al. (2002) |
| Meat and fish industries | Proteinase | To tenderize meat and solubilize fish products | Saha and Demirjian (2000) |

[Amylases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/amylase "Learn more about Amylases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are the most important enzymes in the industrial starch conversion process. Amylolytic enzymes act on starch and related oligo- and [polysaccharides](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/polysaccharide "Learn more about polysaccharides from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (Pandey et al., 2000a). The application of these enzymes has been established in starch [liquefaction](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/liquefaction "Learn more about liquefaction from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), paper, food, sugar, and pharmaceutical industries. In the food industry, amylolytic enzymes have a large scale of applications, such as the production of glucose syrups, [maltose](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/maltose "Learn more about maltose from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") syrup, reduction of viscosity of sugar syrups, to produce clarified fruit juice for longer shelf-life, [solubilization](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/solubilization "Learn more about solubilization from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of starch in the brewing industry (Pandey et al., 2000b). The baking industry uses amylases to delay the staling of bread and other baked products.

The major application of [proteases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/proteinase "Learn more about proteases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in the dairy industry is for the manufacture of cheese. Calf [rennin](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/chymosin "Learn more about rennin from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (chymosin) had been preferred in cheese making due to its high specificity, but microbial proteases are also used. Chymosin is an [aspartic acid protease](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/aspartic-proteinase "Learn more about aspartic acid protease from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") which causes the coagulation of milk. The primary function of these enzymes in cheese making is to [hydrolyze](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/enzymatic-hydrolysis "Learn more about hydrolyze from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") the specific [peptide bond](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/peptide-bond "Learn more about peptide bond from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that generates para-k-casein and macropeptides (Rao et al., 1998). Production of calf rennin (chymosin) in recombinant _A. niger_ var. _awamori_ amounted to about 1 g/L after [nitrosoguanidine](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/nitrosoguanidine "Learn more about nitrosoguanidine from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[mutagenesis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/mutagenesis "Learn more about mutagenesis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and selection for 2-deoxyglucose resistance. Four recombinant proteases have been approved by FDA for cheese production (Bodie et al., 1994; Pariza and Johnson, 2001).

The application of enzymes (proteases, [lipases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/triacylglycerol-lipase "Learn more about lipases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [esterases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/esterase "Learn more about esterases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [lactase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/lactase "Learn more about lactase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and catalase) in [dairy technology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/dairy-technology "Learn more about dairy technology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is well established. [Rennets](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/rennet "Learn more about Rennets from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (rennin) are used for coagulation of milk in the first stage of cheese production. Proteases of various kinds are used for acceleration of [cheese ripening](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/cheese-ripening "Learn more about cheese ripening from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), for modification of functional properties and for modification of [milk proteins](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/food-science/milk-protein "Learn more about milk proteins from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to reduce the allergenic properties of cow [milk products](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/dairy-product "Learn more about milk products from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for infants. [Lipases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/pancreatic-lipase "Learn more about Lipases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are used mainly in cheese ripening for development of lipolytic flavors. Lactase is used to hydrolyze lactose to glucose and [galactose](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/galactose "Learn more about galactose from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") as a digestive aid and to improve the solubility and sweetness in various [dairy products](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/food-science/dairy-product "Learn more about dairy products from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Lactose [hydrolysis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/enzymatic-hydrolysis "Learn more about hydrolysis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") helps these lactose-intolerant people to drink milk and eat various dairy products (Tucker and Woods, 1995). Lactases have also been used in the processing of dairy wastes and as a digestive aid taken by humans in tablet form when consuming dairy products. Recently three novel [xylanases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/xylanase "Learn more about xylanases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") thermophilic in nature (XynA,B,C) have been characterized (Du et al., 2013); these were produced by _[Humicola](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/humicola "Learn more about Humicola from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ sp. for their potential applications in the brewing industry. This XynA also possessed higher [catalytic efficiency](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/catalytic-efficiency "Learn more about catalytic efficiency from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and specificity for a range of substrates.

Proteinases, either indigenous (cathepsin) or those obtained from plants and microorganisms, are used in the meat and fish industries to tenderize meat and solubilize fish products. Tenderization of meat can be achieved by keeping the rapidly chilled meat at 1–2°C to allow [proteolysis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/proteolysis "Learn more about proteolysis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") by indigenous enzymes. Enzymes are also used to facilitate separation of hemoglobin from [blood proteins](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/blood-proteins "Learn more about blood proteins from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and removal of meat from bones. For the preparation of [pet food](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/pet-foods "Learn more about pet food from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [minced meat](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/food-science/minced-meat "Learn more about minced meat from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or meat by-products are hydrolyzed by proteases to produce a liquid meat digest or slurry with a much lower viscosity (Saha and Demirjian, 2000). Fish protein concentrates are generally prepared by treating ground fish parts with a [protease](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/proteinase "Learn more about protease from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

In the baking industry, there is an increasing focus on lipolytic enzymes. Recent findings suggest that [phospholipases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/phospholipase "Learn more about phospholipases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") can be used to substitute or supplement traditional [emulsifiers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/emulsifying-agent "Learn more about emulsifiers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), as the enzymes degrade polar wheat [lipids](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/lipid "Learn more about lipids from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to produce emulsifying lipids. Also, research is currently devoted towards the further understanding of bread staling and the mechanisms behind the enzymatic prevention of staling in presence of α-amylases and xylanases (Andreu et al., 1999). Lipases are commonly used in the production of a variety of products, ranging from fruit juices, baked foods, and vegetable fermentations. Fats, oils, and related compounds are the main targets of lipases in food technology. Accurate control of [lipase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/triacylglycerol-lipase "Learn more about lipase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") concentration, pH, temperature, and emulsion content is required to maximize the production of flavor and fragrance. The lipase mediation of carbohydrate esters of fatty acids offers a potential market for use as emulsifiers in foods and pharmaceuticals. There are three recombinant fungal lipases currently used in the food industry, one from _[Rhizomucor](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/rhizomucor "Learn more about Rhizomucor from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ _miehi_, one from _[Thermomyces lanuginosus](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/thermomyces-lanuginosus "Learn more about Thermomyces lanuginosus from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_, and another from _[Fusarium oxysporum](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/fusarium-oxysporum "Learn more about Fusarium oxysporum from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ (Mendez and Salas, 2001).

Enzymes can play important roles in preparing and processing various fruit and vegetable juices, such as apple, orange, grapefruit, pineapple, carrot, lemon etc. Fruits and vegetables are particularly rich in pectic substances. Pectin, a [hydrocolloid](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/hydrocolloid "Learn more about hydrocolloid from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), has a great affinity for water and can form gels under certain conditions. The addition of pectinases, [pectin lyase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/pectin-lyase "Learn more about pectin lyase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), pectin [esterase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/esterase "Learn more about esterase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and polygalacturanase, reduces viscosity and improves pressability as the pectin gel collapses (Tucker and Woods, 1995). For complete liquefaction of fruits and vegetable juices, hemicellulases and amylases can be used with pectinases. [Flavoprotein](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/flavoprotein "Learn more about Flavoprotein from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") glucose [oxidase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/oxidase "Learn more about oxidase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), is used to scavenge oxygen in fruit juice and beverages to prevent color and taste changes upon storage. [Glucose oxidase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/glucose-oxidase "Learn more abou

--- SOURCE 2: Industrial enzyme applications ---
URL: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/12323357/

SUMMARY:
by O Kirk · 2002 · Cited by 2104 — The effective catalytic properties of enzymes have already promoted their introduction into several industrial products and processes.

# Industrial enzyme applications

# Industrial enzyme applications

## Abstract

The effective catalytic properties of enzymes have already promoted their introduction into several industrial products and processes. Recent developments in biotechnology, particularly in areas such as protein engineering and directed evolution, have provided important tools for the efficient development of new enzymes. This has resulted in the development of enzymes with improved properties for established technical applications and in the production of new enzymes tailor-made for entirely new areas of application where enzymes have not previously been used.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Industrial enzymes exhibit specific catalytic properties, thermotolerance, and stability under extreme conditions, making them ideal for diverse applications in food, pharmaceuticals, and bioenergy.

Advanced techniques like protein engineering and directed evolution enable the development of customized enzymes with enhanced performance, specificity, and stability for targeted industrial applications.
Industrial enzymes are widely used across sectors, including food processing, pharmaceuticals, textiles, and detergents, enhancing product quality and environmental sustainability.
Challenges include enzyme stability, high production costs, and regulatory hurdles, which can impede the widespread adoption of engineered enzymes in various industries.
Future trends point towards increased use of enzymes in green chemistry, biocatalysis, and personalized medicine, driven by advancements in biotechnology and sustainability initiatives.
